attributes.stem:
A 29-year-old primigravida woman at 17 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department late one evening with a seven-hour history of right upper quadrant pain. She reports two similar episodes over the last month, each time following a large meal, and on both occasions her pain resolved within three hours.

On examination her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse 100/min, and temperature 37.8°C. Her abdomen is soft apart from tenderness in the right subcostal region. An ultrasound examination shows several gallstones and a thickened gallbladder.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Supportive management now and expectant management post-partum
Supportive management with serial ultrasound to detect complications
attributes.explanation:
During pregnancy, women are at increased risk of gallbladder disease due to increased gallbladder stasis and production of bile with decreased chenodeoxycholic acid and increased cholesterol. This woman presents with an episode of cholecystitis, suggested by her prolonged right upper quadrant pain, low-grade fever and right upper quadrant tenderness. She also has a history suggestive of two prior episodes of biliary colic. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the current admission is recommended because it reduces the risk of recurrent symptoms and complications such as acute cholecystitis or gallstone pancreatitis, which are associated with high maternal-fetal risks. Evidence shows that conservative management is linked with significantly higher maternal-fetal complications, including preterm birth and low fetal growth. According to guidelines, laparoscopic cholecystectomy can be safely performed during any trimester.

Cholecystectomy immediately post-partum or six weeks post-partum delays definitive treatment, increasing the risk of recurrent symptoms and complications during pregnancy, which can be detrimental to both the mother and fetus.

Supportive management now and expectant management post-partum is not advisable due to the high likelihood of recurrent symptoms and potential complications during pregnancy, which can lead to adverse outcomes.

Supportive management with serial ultrasound to detect complications does not address the underlying issue and leaves the patient at risk for further episodes of cholecystitis or other complications, which can be more challenging to manage during pregnancy.
learningPoints: Operative treatment for acute cholecystitis during pregnancy is associated with better outcomes compared with conservative management.
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy can be safely performed during any trimester of pregnancy.
references: İlhan M, İlhan G, Gök AFK, Günay K, Ertekin C. The course and outcomes of complicated gallstone disease in pregnancy: Experience of a tertiary center. Turk J Obstet Gynecol. 2016 Dec;13(4):178-182. doi: 10.4274/tjod.65475. Epub 2016 Dec 15. PMID: 28913118; PMCID: PMC5558289. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28913118/

Pearl JP, Price RR, Tonkin AE, Richardson WS, Stefanidis D. SAGES guidelines for the use of laparoscopy during pregnancy. Surg Endosc. 2017 Oct;31(10):3767-3782. doi: 10.1007/s00464-017-5637-3. Epub 2017 Jun 22. PMID: 28643072. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28643072/

Rios-Diaz AJ, Oliver EA, Bevilacqua LA, Metcalfe D, Yeo CJ, Berghella V, Palazzo F. Is It Safe to Manage Acute Cholecystitis Nonoperatively During Pregnancy?: A Nationwide Analysis of Morbidity According to Management Strategy. Ann Surg. 2020 Sep 1;272(3):449-456. doi: 10.1097/SLA.0000000000004210. Erratum in: Ann Surg. 2021 May 1;273(5):e184-e187. doi: 10.1097/SLA.0000000000004786. PMID: 33759834. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33759834/

Pearl J, Price R, Richardson W, Fanelli R; Society of American Gastrointestinal Endoscopic Surgeons. Guidelines for diagnosis, treatment, and use of laparoscopy for surgical problems during pregnancy. Surg Endosc. 2011 Nov;25(11):3479-92. doi: 10.1007/s00464-011-1927-3. Epub 2011 Sep 23. PMID: 21938570. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21938570/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
